25 years and counting

Destination
Reservation

Every October, a small crew loads a U-Haul with over a thousand handmade Christmas stockings and drives thousands of miles to deliver them to Native American reservation communities.

The Trip

Nobody gets paid.
Nobody takes a cut.

It starts in Alabama. The stockings are packed by hand — by families, by church groups, by people who sew through the summer so a child they'll never meet has something to open in December.

Then someone drives a U-Haul. Through Arkansas in the dark. Across Kansas in 40-mile crosswinds. Up mountain passes with steep grades and tight curves. Into reservations in Oklahoma, South Dakota, and North Dakota — wherever the stockings are needed and the people are waiting.

In 2024, the crew delivered over 1,200 stockings — a new record. The trip has been running for over 25 years, led by Barbara Rimes, who has made this drive for the better part of three decades.

Why it matters

Reservation communities face some of the most severe poverty in the United States. Unemployment, inadequate housing, and limited access to basic services are daily realities for many families. Native American children experience rates of poverty, food insecurity, and housing instability that far exceed the national average.

The crisis goes deeper. Alcoholism devastates families across generations. Teenage suicide rates on many reservations run several times higher than the national average — among the highest of any population in the country. These are children carrying weight that no child should have to carry.

A Christmas stocking doesn't fix any of that. But it says something that matters: someone drove a very long way to bring you this. You were worth the trip.

The Work, in Numbers

No staff. No office. No overhead. Every dollar donated buys supplies that go directly into the stockings.

1,200+
Stockings in 2024
25+
Years on the road
100%
Volunteer run

What goes in every stocking

Basic hygiene supplies. Warm socks. Small toys chosen with care for children from infants up to age 16. Not a mass donation sorted by weight — each stocking is made by hand, by someone who packed it thinking about a specific child in a specific place.

We deliver to the Choctaw Nation in Oklahoma. To the Yankton Sioux, the Lower Brule, and Standing Rock in South Dakota. To the Spirit Lake Nation in North Dakota. To Pine Ridge. To wherever people open the door and let us in.

"533 miles today. Lots of corn and sorghum. Tomorrow will be a long day."
From the trip diary, Day 3

Questions

Frequently Asked

Everything you want to know before you give or volunteer.

Where does my donation actually go? +
Every dollar goes toward stocking supplies and truck rental. Volunteers pay for their own gas, food, and hotels out of pocket. There is no staff, no office, and no administrative overhead. If you send us money, it buys what goes in the stockings or gets the stockings there.
Who runs this? +
Destination Reservation is an all-volunteer operation. Barbara Rimes has led the trip for over 25 years. A small crew of volunteers makes the drive each October. Everyone involved does this on their own time and their own dime.
Where do the stockings go? +
We deliver to multiple reservation communities across Oklahoma, South Dakota, and North Dakota — including the Choctaw Nation, the Yankton Sioux, the Lower Brule, Standing Rock, Spirit Lake, and Pine Ridge. The list has grown over the years as relationships have developed with community contacts on each reservation.
Who receives the stockings? +
The stockings are made for children from infants up to age 16. We work with community contacts on each reservation to make sure they reach the families and children who need them most.
Can I volunteer? +
Yes, and there are two ways to help. You can join the road trip each October — just know that volunteers cover all their own travel expenses. Or you can help from home by shopping for supplies or assembling stockings to be picked up before the trip. Either way, reach out through the contact form below.
Can I donate stockings instead of money? +
Yes. If you want to assemble and donate stockings directly, we'd love the help. Be aware that mailing stockings is not practical — shipping 70 stockings just 400 miles costs close to $2,000. The economics only work because everything travels together in one truck. Contact us to arrange a pickup or drop-off.
Is this a religious organization? +
No. Destination Reservation is non-denominational. We work with churches on the reservations because they are often the most effective way to reach the families who need help most — not because of any shared theology. The churches we partner with represent different denominations and traditions. We don't discriminate, and neither do they.
Why are the stockings in trash bags in the photos? +
Two reasons. Black trash bags keep the stockings dry during transport — the truck isn't climate controlled and we drive through all kinds of weather. They also disguise the contents. Crime rates on reservation lands are high, and a truck full of visible Christmas gifts is a target. The bags protect the stockings until they're in the right hands.

Support the Trip

Your money buys supplies.
That's the whole deal.

No development director. No gala. Just a garage in Alabama, some Christmas fabric, a truck, and a long list of miles to drive. Send what you can and it goes directly into the stockings.
